name: Italian Bee
description: The world's most popular honeybee! Golden-yellow with gentle temperament and excellent honey production. Originally from the Italian peninsula.
diet: Nectar and pollen from diverse flowers
funFact: Italian bees are so gentle that many beekeepers work them without gloves!
culturalNote: Brought to North America in the 1850s, Italian bees quickly became the favorite of American beekeepers due to their calm nature.
superpower: Prolific Egg Layer — queens can lay 2,000+ eggs per day!

name: Carniolan Bee
description: Dark gray with light bands, from the Alps. Extremely gentle, winter-hardy, and known for explosive spring buildup. Perfect for cold climates!
diet: Nectar and pollen, conservative winter consumers
funFact: Carniolan bees form tiny winter clusters to save food — they're the ultimate conservationists!
culturalNote: Slovenia considers the Carniolan bee a national treasure and has strict laws protecting its genetic purity.
superpower: Winter Warrior — survives harsh alpine winters with minimal food stores!

name: Russian Bee
description: Bred from bees in Russia's Far East that co-evolved with Varroa mites for 150+ years. Natural mite resistance!
habitat: Managed apiaries, especially where mite pressure is high
funFact: Russian bees constantly build queen cells even when happy — this confuses new beekeepers!
culturalNote: USDA imported Russian bees to the US in 1997 specifically to combat the Varroa mite crisis.
superpower: Mite Fighter — natural Varroa resistance through 150 years of co-evolution!

name: Buckfast Bee
description: Created by Brother Adam at Buckfast Abbey over 70 years. Gentle, productive, and resistant to tracheal mites.
diet: Nectar and pollen from diverse sources
funFact: Brother Adam traveled over 100,000 miles across 5 continents collecting bee genetics!
culturalNote: Brother Adam was a Benedictine monk who spent his entire 98-year life perfecting this hybrid.
superpower: Designer Bee — combines the best traits of dozens of subspecies!

name: German Dark Bee
description: The original European honeybee — dark brown to black. Hardy and thrifty. First bee brought to North America!
funFact: Brought to Jamestown, Virginia in 1622 — Native Americans called them 'white man's flies'!
culturalNote: Nearly extinct in pure form. Conservation programs in Scandinavia are preserving this historic subspecies.
superpower: The Original — 10,000+ years of adaptation to cold Northern European climates!

name: Africanized Bee
description: A hybrid that escaped a Brazilian lab in 1957. Incredibly productive but extremely defensive — the 'killer bees'.
funFact: They swarm up to 16 times per year and can chase a threat for over a quarter mile!
culturalNote: Despite their reputation, Africanized bees are the primary managed pollinators in Brazil.
superpower: Ferocious Defenders — will deploy the entire colony to protect the hive!

name: Caucasian Bee
description: From the Caucasus Mountains. Famous for having the longest tongue of any honeybee!
funFact: Caucasian bees use more propolis than any other subspecies — they cement their hive shut!
culturalNote: Georgian beekeeping dates back over 5,000 years. The Caucasian bee is a national symbol.
superpower: Long Tongue — can reach nectar that other bees literally can't!

name: Cordovan Bee
description: A golden color variant of the Italian bee with no dark pigmentation. Bright orange-gold all over!
funFact: So distinctively colored that it's super easy to spot the queen — she's the big bright gold one!
culturalNote: A favorite of queen breeders and backyard beekeepers for their beauty and gentle nature.
superpower: Golden Beauty — the most visually striking honeybee variant in the world!

name: Queen Bee
description: The mother of the entire colony. Only she can lay fertilized eggs. Lives 2-5 years!
funFact: She mates with 15-20 drones mid-air and stores all the sperm she'll ever need for her entire life!
culturalNote: For centuries people called her the 'King Bee' — they didn't realize she was female until the 1600s!
superpower: Royal Mother — lays 2,000 eggs/day and controls the colony through pheromones!

name: Nurse Bee
description: Young workers who feed and care for larvae. They check each larva up to 1,300 times a day!
diet: Honey and pollen (produces royal jelly for larvae)
funFact: Nurse bees check on each larva up to 1,300 times per day — the most attentive babysitters in nature!
culturalNote: The discovery that worker bees change jobs as they age was made by Gustav Roesch in 1927.
superpower: Royal Jelly Factory — special head glands produce the superfood that feeds all larvae!

name: Forager Bee
description: The oldest workers who fly out to collect nectar, pollen, water, and propolis. Navigate by the sun!
habitat: Fields, gardens, orchards (up to 3 miles from hive)
funFact: Visits 50-100 flowers per trip, 10-12 trips per day — that's 1,200 flowers daily by one bee!
culturalNote: Forager bees measure distance by tracking how fast the landscape moves past their eyes during flight.
superpower: Navigator — uses the sun, polarized light, and magnetic fields to find flowers miles away!
region: Up to 3 miles radius from hive

name: Guard Bee
description: Stationed at the hive entrance, guards check every bee's scent to verify colony membership.
funFact: Guard bees can smell the difference between a sister and a stranger — each colony has a unique chemical 'passport'!
culturalNote: Guard bees release alarm pheromone to recruit reinforcements — this is why beekeepers smoke the entrance first.
superpower: Colony Bouncer — checks every bee's chemical ID before allowing entry!

name: Scout Bee
description: Elite foragers who search for new food sources and new homes for swarms. Only ~5% of foragers become scouts!
funFact: Scout bees have different brain chemistry than other foragers — they're literally wired for adventure!
culturalNote: When a colony swarms, scouts evaluate new homes and the colony 'votes' by joining dances — bee democracy!
superpower: Thrill-Seeker — genetically wired for exploration and risk-taking!

name: Drone Bee
description: The males — bigger, louder, with enormous eyes. Their only job is mating. No stinger, can't forage!
funFact: Drones that successfully mate die instantly — mating is literally their final act!
culturalNote: In fall, workers drag drones out and refuse to let them back in — 'drone eviction' saves food for winter.
superpower: Sky King — enormous eyes spot a queen from 30+ feet during high-speed mating flights!
region: Inside the hive and drone congregation areas

name: Honey
description: Nectar transformed by enzymes and evaporation into a supersaturated sugar solution that never spoils!
funFact: 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs was still perfectly edible!
culturalNote: Honey was used as currency in ancient Germany, as medicine in Egypt, and as offerings to gods worldwide.
superpower: Eternal Food — naturally antibacterial, it's the only food that never spoils!

name: Beeswax
description: Young workers secrete wax scales from abdomen glands, then chew and mold them into hexagonal comb.
funFact: It takes 8 pounds of honey to produce 1 pound of beeswax — that's why beekeepers reuse comb!
culturalNote: Beeswax candles were reserved for churches and royalty in medieval Europe.
superpower: Perfect Architecture — hexagonal cells use the least wax per unit of storage!

name: Propolis
scientificName: Plant resins mixed with wax and enzymes
description: Sticky tree resins mixed with wax and enzymes — a powerful antimicrobial 'bee glue' that sterilizes the hive.
funFact: If a mouse enters and dies, bees mummify it in propolis to prevent disease!
culturalNote: Hippocrates used propolis to heal wounds 2,400 years ago. Modern research confirms its antimicrobial properties.
superpower: Antimicrobial Shield — sterilizes the hive and fights bacteria, viruses, and fungi!

name: Royal Jelly
description: Milky-white superfood from nurse bees' head glands. All larvae eat it for 3 days, but only queen larvae eat it exclusively — creating a queen!
funFact: Royal jelly rewires bee DNA through epigenetics — the same egg becomes a 6-week worker or a 5-year queen based solely on diet!
culturalNote: One of the most expensive bee products — harvesting yields only tiny amounts per colony.
superpower: Queen Maker — the epigenetic superfood that transforms a regular egg into royalty!

name: Bee Pollen
description: Foragers pack flower pollen into pellets on their back legs. The colony's protein source for raising brood!
habitat: Pollen cells around the brood nest
funFact: Bee pollen comes in every color of the rainbow — purple from phacelia, bright orange from dandelion!
culturalNote: Some beekeepers install 'pollen traps' that gently scrape pellets off returning foragers' legs.
superpower: Rainbow Protein — each pellet contains up to 200,000 individual pollen grains!

name: Bee Venom
description: A complex cocktail of proteins and peptides. Painful when stung, but studied for medical applications!
habitat: Worker bee venom sac (connected to stinger)
funFact: The main compound (melittin) is being researched as a treatment for HIV, arthritis, and even some cancers!
culturalNote: Apitherapy — using bee stings as medicine — has been practiced for thousands of years.
superpower: Medical Marvel — compounds being studied to fight HIV, cancer, and autoimmune diseases!
